evidence_quotes.quote: games that are required to always be online.

end of life plan, which basically means that they either remove the online connectivity requirement for these games, or make the servers publically available to the players, so that they can keep playing the game they spent money on.

paragraphs.text: I'd like you to consider protections for video games, specifically games that are required to always be online. Imagine if you buy a new kitchen appliance, or a car, etc. and when the company that made it creates a new model or goes out of business. your purchase just stops working and is gone forever. It's not broken or anything, someone else just decided that you can't use it anymore. I, along with MANY others in the Stop Killing Games movement, would like companies that sell games in the EU, that either you have to pay for to play, or have in-game purchases, to be required to implement an end of life plan, which basically means that they either remove the online connectivity requirement for these games, or make the servers publically available to the players, so that they can keep playing the game they spent money on, and the producer/publisher can wash their hands off of it and move on to other things.
